title: Get started with datey
text: is_proper() returns TRUE start ≤ end; is_collapsed() returns TRUE start ≥ end. point interval [, ) proper collapsed (contains time):
code: is_proper(interval)    # TRUE because start <= end #> [1] TRUE is_collapsed(interval) # FALSE because start < end #> [1] FALSE  pt <- a %to% a         # empty (point) interval is_proper(pt)          # TRUE because a <= a #> [1] TRUE is_collapsed(pt)       # TRUE because a >= a #> [1] TRUE

title: Get started with datey
text: & operator returns intersection two datey_intervals. direct way compute overlap two time periods:
code: period    <- start_day(2023, 7, 1) %to% end_day(2024, 6, 30) year_2024 <- start_day(2024, 1, 1) %to% end_day(2024, 12, 31)  overlap <- period & year_2024 overlap #> [1] [2024-01-01.0, 2024-07-01.0)  overlap$duration  # exposure in calendar year 2024, in years #> [1] 0.497268 yr

what: The datey approach: a fixed annual grid
title: Why datey?
text: datey picks one standardised, precise mapping dates onto annual grid, guarantees arithmetic grid exact associative. Every datey durationy stored internally count clicks, one click 1 / 534 360 year, number chosen 1/365 1/366 year, useful fractions days years, represented exactly. approach, date duration calculations reduce plain old integer arithmetic precise associative. practical consequence two-steps-vs-one-step problem simply arise: (+ d1) + d2 == + (d1 + d2) datey durationys d1, d2 – always, exactly, regardless leap years order operations. ’s guarantee datey built around, specification sets precisely.
code: start <- start_day(2000, 1, 1)  half_year <- durationy(0.5) two_steps <- (start + half_year) + half_year one_step  <- start + (half_year + half_year)  two_steps #> [1] 2001-01-01.0 one_step #> [1] 2001-01-01.0 identical(two_steps, one_step) #> [1] TRUE

title: Why datey?
text: Actuarial calculations often involve asking “much period rate X apply?” – e.g. combining policy’s time risk period particular assumption set valid. datey represents time intervals datey_intervals, written start %% end. half-open, .e. [start, end), intervals, means consecutive periods interlock precisely without gaps double-counting. datey provides interval algebra work time intervals directly:
code: time_at_risk <- start_day(2023, 4, 1) %to% end_day(2024, 4, 1) rate_period_2024 <- start_day(2024, 1, 1) %to% end_day(2025, 12, 31)  # the part of the time at risk to which the 2024 rate applies overlap <- time_at_risk & rate_period_2024 overlap #> [1] [2024-01-01.0, 2024-04-02.0)  # ... as a duration in years, ready to multiply by an annual rate durationy(overlap$end - overlap$start) #> [1] 0.251366 yr

title: Exact Date and Duration Arithmetic on an Annual Grid
text: datey package provides standardised mapping dates onto discrete annual grid, together exact date duration arithmetic. work primarily mortality rates time intervals defined year input data uses dates, precision important, worth considering using datey. Classic examples mortality experience analysis valuation life assurance annuities. Mortality rates defined per annum experience valuation data usually defined using dates (.e. days). benefits using datey : Standardised mapping dates uniform annual timescale. unique way map dates annual timescale. Without standardisation, sooner later errors arise.1 Fixed precision arithmetic. Using fixed precision excludes common bugs arising imprecision floating point arithmetic.2 Handling whether date means start, end day. issue usually ignored3, systematic errors can accumulate end material. detail motivation datey, see datey?.

title: Exact Date and Duration Arithmetic on an Annual Grid
text: hands-guide provided Get started.
code: library(datey)  # Create datey from a base R date ... start <- start_day(as.Date("2018-09-12")) # Exposure includes the whole day # ... or direct from year/month/day exit <- mid_day(2024, 3, 7) # Death is mid-day on average # ... or as a fractional calendar year (which will be rounded to grid precision) t <- datey(2021.234) # A date in the middle of the exposure period  c(start, t, exit) # 2018-09-12.0 2021-03-27.4098 2024-03-07.5 #> [1] 2018-09-12.0    2021-03-27.4098 2024-03-07.5  interval <- start %to% exit # Exposure period interval # [2018-09-12.0, 2024-03-07.5) #> [1] [2018-09-12.0, 2024-03-07.5)  interval %includes% t # Test if a point in time is in an interval #> [1] TRUE  exit - start      # Duration of exposure period #> [1] 5.485804 yr interval$duration # Same thing #> [1] 5.485804 yr  interval$duration * 0.01 # Converted to years when mixed with plain numbers #> [1] 0.05485804

c(start_day(c_date), mid_day(c_date), end_day(c_date)) #> [1] 2001-02-03.0 2001-02-03.5 2001-02-04.0 # An R datetime implies a position within a day: datey(c_date) # 2001-02-03.5 #> [1] 2001-02-03.5  # Use `strict` to control error behaviour for invalid years: try(end_day(0999, 12, 31)) #> [1] 1000-01-01.0 try(datey(3000.1)) #> Error : Year must be in [1000, 3000]. end_day(0999, 12, 31, strict = FALSE) #> [1] 1000-01-01.0 datey(3000.1, strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA>  # NAs are passed through regardless of `strict` # (provided they are numeric) end_day(NA_real_, 12, 31, strict = TRUE) #> [1] <NA> datey(NA_real_, strict = FALSE) #> [1] <NA>  # Text: start_day("2001-02-03") #> [1] 2001-02-03.0 mid_day("2001-02-03") #> [1] 2001-02-03.5 end_day("2001-02-03") #> [1] 2001-02-04.0 datey("2001-02-03") #> [1] 2001-02-03.0 datey("2001-02-03.0") #> [1] 2001-02-03.0 datey("2001-02-03", day_fraction = 0) #> [1] 2001-02-03.0 datey("2001-02-03.5") #> [1] 2001-02-03.5 datey("2001-02-03", day_fraction = 0.5) #> [1] 2001-02-03.5  # Text round trips: t <- datey(2001.234) identical(t, datey(as.character(t))) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE  # Day fraction cannot be present # both in the text and as an argument # implicitly or explicitly: try(start_day("2001-02-03.0")) #> Error : Invalid datey text. Should be "YYYY-MM-DD" (no fractional part). try(datey("2001-02-03.0", day_fraction = 0)) #> Error : Invalid datey text. Should be "YYYY-MM-DD" (no fractional part).  # Handling blanks: try(start_day("")) #> Error : Blank datey text (and blank_is_NA is FALSE). start_day("", blank_is_NA = TRUE) #> [1] <NA>  # Invalids: try(mid_day("abc")) #> Error : Invalid datey text. title: Operators for datey, durationy and datey_interval — ops
code: t_2000 <- datey(2000) t_2001 <- datey(2001) d_0.5 <- durationy(0.5)  t_2000 #> [1] 2000-01-01.0 t_2001 #> [1] 2001-01-01.0 d_0.5 #> [1] 0.5 yr  t_2001 - t_2000 # `datey` - `datey` is a `durationy` #> [1] 1 yr t_2000 + d_0.5  # `datey` + `durationy` is a `datey` #> [1] 2000-07-02.0 t_2001 - d_0.5  # `datey` - `durationy` is a `datey` #> [1] 2000-07-02.0 t_2000 + 0.5    # Arithmetic with numerics results in a double #> [1] 2000.5 d_0.5 + d_0.5   # `durationy` + `durationy` is a `durationy` #> [1] 1 yr d_0.5 + 0.5     # Arithmetic with numerics results in a double #> [1] 1 d_0.5 * 2       # Arithmetic with numerics results in a double #> [1] 1  interval <- t_2000 %to% t_2001 interval #> [1] [2000-01-01.0, 2001-01-01.0)  # %to% also accepts numbers: 2000 %to% 2001 #> [1] [2000-01-01.0, 2001-01-01.0)  interval %includes% t_2000 # TRUE -- start *is* included in an interval #> [1] TRUE interval %includes% (t_2000 + d_0.5) # TRUE #> [1] TRUE interval %includes% t_2001 # FALSE -- end is *not* included in an interval #> [1] FALSE  # %includes% also accepts a number as its right hand operand: interval %includes% 2000.5 #> [1] TRUE  # %includes% handling of NAs: interval %includes% NA_datey_         # FALSE (not NA) #> [1] FALSE NA_datey_interval_ %includes% t_2000  # FALSE (not NA) #> [1] FALSE  (2000 %to% 2020) & (2010 %to% 2030) # [2010-01-01.0, 2020-01-01.0) #> [1] [2010-01-01.0, 2020-01-01.0)  # Non-intersecting *adjacent* intervals: (2000 %to% 2001) & (2001 %to% 2002) # [2001-01-01.0, 2001-01-01.0) #> [1] [2001-01-01.0, 2001-01-01.0) # Non-intersecting *non*-adjacent intervals: (1900 %to% 1901) & (2001 %to% 2001) # <NA> #> [1] <NA>

title: datey 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release. datey durationy represent dates durations discrete annual grid, exact, associative arithmetic (+, -, comparisons, mean(), range(), etc.). start_day(), mid_day(), end_day() datey() map calendar year/month/day (day-fraction), fractional years Date, POSIXct, POSIXlt datey. to_ymdf() $year, $month, $day $day_fraction provide breakdown datey year, month, day day fraction components. is_start_day() is_mid_day() provides checking datey alignment. .double() .integer() provide convenient conversion numeric types years, mixed number datey/durationy arithmetic adhering convention using implicit conversion. NA_datey_, NA_durationy_ NA_datey_interval_ provide type-correct NAs, full .na()/anyNA() support. Text parsing formatting datey, durationy datey_interval. pillar_shaft() methods readable printing datey/durationy/ datey_interval columns tibbles. is_leap_year() integer years dateys. == != equality comparison. %includes% (interval_includes()) test whether interval contains datey numeric value. & intersection: returns empty collapsed interval adjacent intervals, NA_datey_interval_ non-intersecting non-adjacent intervals. is_collapsed(), is_proper() related predicates. Constants all_of_time NA_datey_interval_. seq() supported datey durationy via argument (note: length.supported).
